old_claim.note: Iron Working provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Iron working is not part of the evidence-backed direct boundary for the timber-hulled, oar-powered Greek trireme and its bronze ram.

source_shape.source_locator: Construction account, paragraphs 1-4: Olympias was built by ship carpenters with a timber keel, planking and frames, wooden wedges, brass nails, and a cast brass ram cap.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Hellenic Navy's full-scale reconstruction documents a complete timber-and-copper-alloy construction system without an iron-specific structural requirement.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The former citation concerned Egyptian meteoritic beads rather than shipbuilding; the functioning reconstruction instead identifies wood and copper-alloy components as the relevant material technologies.

ontology_before: An unrelated early iron-artifact tradition was treated as a direct cause of Greek trireme construction.
ontology_after: Iron working is excluded unless future trireme-specific structural evidence establishes an indispensable iron component.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-greek-trireme-boundary: iron_working is absent.
would_reject_if: Trireme-specific archaeological or reconstruction evidence shows that an iron component was indispensable to the hull, propulsion system, rig, or ram.
short_reason: The old meteoritic-bead source was unrelated, while the reconstructed trireme architecture does not require iron working.
